blurb: The cottage-garden cornflower — intense true-blue (or pink/white) fringed buttons on wiry grey-green stems, an easy cool-season annual that reseeds and draws bees by the dozen.
water: Low to moderate; tolerates drought once established
spread: Clumping; reseeds and naturalizes readily where it's grown
seasons: Blue (or pink/white) fringed flowers late spring into summer; grey-green foliage spring–summer; fades and dies at frost
wildlife: A magnet for bees and butterflies; the blooms carry nectaries that draw beneficial insects, and seedheads feed finches and other small birds
deer: Generally deer-resistant — the wiry, grey-felted stems are usually passed over
toxic: Non-toxic — reported safe around horses, dogs, and cats; the petals are a traditional edible garnish
habitat: Cornfields, disturbed grassland, roadsides, and waste ground

care.hardiness: Grown as a cool-season hardy annual — seedlings tolerate light frost and fall-sown plants overwinter in milder spots; it completes its life in one season and is finished by hard frost. It blooms best in the cool of late spring and early summer and declines in midsummer heat.[1][3]
care.planting: Direct-sow early — bachelor's button likes cool soil and resents transplanting. Sow in the garden as soon as the ground can be worked in spring (a few weeks before the mid-May last frost; it tolerates light frost), or sow in fall to overwinter for an early, stronger bloom. In containers, sow into a deep, free-draining pot to allow root run; pots dry quickly in Front Range heat and wind, so water consistently. It finishes at frost with no overwintering.[1][2][3]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours keeps stems upright and free-flowering; in too much shade the stems flop and bloom thins.[1][3]
care.soil: Adaptable and undemanding — tolerates poor, lean soils and any texture (clay, loam, or sand) across a wide pH from acidic to alkaline, so Boulder's alkaline clay suits it. It wants well-drained ground and dislikes waterlogging.[1][3]
care.water: Low to moderate — keep the seedbed evenly moist until plants establish, then water sparingly; it tolerates drought once rooted in. Avoid overwatering, which produces weak, floppy growth.[1][3]
care.spacing: Thin or space plants about 6–12 in apart; sow in drifts for the best cottage-garden and cutting effect.[1]
care.propagation: Seed only — this annual cannot be divided or rooted from cuttings. Direct-sow in fall (mild spots) or start indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost, though direct sowing is more reliable. It reseeds readily, so an established patch returns yearly; collect the bristly seeds from dried heads to sow where wanted.[1][3]
care.depth: Sow seed about ¼–½ in deep — it germinates better with darkness over the seed — and keep moist until it sprouts in roughly 1–2 weeks.[1]
care.bloom: Late spring into summer (roughly May–July on the Front Range), heaviest in cool early-summer weather and shortening as heat builds; deadheading and successive sowings extend the season.[1][3]
care.feeding: Little to none — lean soil keeps plants sturdy. Excess nitrogen produces lax stems and fewer flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead regularly to prolong bloom and limit reseeding; tall types may lean and appreciate a twiggy support or close planting. Pull spent plants in midsummer and let a few heads ripen if you want volunteers.[1][3]
care.selfsow: Reseeds and naturalizes readily where seed drops — welcome volunteers appear the next year. Note: this annual cornflower is NOT a Colorado noxious weed (the listed Centaurea are the knapweeds — diffuse, spotted, and yellow starthistle), but it does reseed freely, so deadhead before seed set if you want to contain it. Don't confuse it with the perennial, rhizome-running mountain bluet (Centaurea montana).[1][3]
care.troubles: Generally easy. Powdery mildew, rust, aphids, and stem/root rot in wet or crowded conditions are the main issues; full sun, lean well-drained soil, and good airflow keep it clean.[1]
care.companions: A cottage- and cutting-garden classic — pairs with other cool-season annuals (larkspur, calendula, nigella, poppies) and pollinator perennials that share its full-sun, lean-soil, self-sowing habit.[1]

edible.summary: Cornflower petals are a long-standing edible garnish — mild, faintly sweet-to-spicy, and prized for their vivid true-blue color on salads, cakes, and as a dried tea/confetti.[1][3]
edible.parts: The flower petals (ray and disk florets) are the edible part, pulled from the bitter green base. They are used fresh to scatter brilliant blue color over salads, desserts, and drinks, and dried as a colorful component of herbal-tea blends (a signature ingredient of Earl Grey-style blends). The flavor is mild — faintly clove-like to slightly bitter — so they are valued more for color than taste.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use only the petals, separated from the green calyx (which is bitter), as a fresh garnish or dried for tea and confetti. Use pesticide-free flowers grown for eating. Treat them as a decorative garnish, not a bulk ingredient.[3]
edible.caution: Cornflower is non-toxic and reported safe around horses, dogs, and cats, but two cautions apply: people allergic to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants — ragweed, chrysanthemum, marigold — may react to it, and as with any edible flower you should eat only pesticide-free blooms in moderation. Use the petals only; the green base is bitter. Do not confuse the edible annual cornflower (Centaurea cyanus) with the noxious knapweeds in the same genus, which are not grown for food.[1]
